turns.text: All of these witnesses are subject to prosecution, and all of them are interviewed initially by the Border Patrol. I thank the Court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Do you have anything further, Mr. Phillips?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Just a few remarks, Mr. Chief Justice. Initially, I've point out from the record that Romero-Morales was asked whether he had heard any testimony or any statements made to the driver by any of the illegal aliens who had been deported. That's on page 44, the joint appendix as to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: How did he answer?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: No, both in terms of the car and then at the house prior to --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: But you didn't hear him. Did that mean that there weren't any?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Did -- he just said he didn't hear any statements made, but he was in their presence and if you're inside a car and they were together in the same room of the house --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: You said that was page 44?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, at the bottom. With regard to the reasonableness of the practice in the Southern District to California, it sounds good when you talk about individualized cases and the step-by-step determination. But it seems to me that the aggregate effect of the rule in the Southern District of California is best demonstrated by the statistics. Five thousand illegal aliens held in custody in order for 36 trials, and we don't even know of those 36 trials, how many if any illegal aliens ever testified on behalf of a defendant.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: On that point, Mr. Phillips, that -- that page 30 of the read brief, they give some figures about the number of cases and they say there were only 11 Mendez-Rodriguez motions filed in a total of 239 cases and only one motion was granted. Is that -- is that accurate?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: We -- we did not independently evaluate that; although, we wouldn't be terribly surprised if there aren't a lot of Mendez-Rodriguez cases in light of the fact that the Government's practice is typically to detain all illegal aliens and/or to dismiss the charges. So you wouldn't expect there'd be very many cases. It doesn't come up because we assume --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I'm assuming this case is an atypical case then. The Government deviated from its normal practice.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: In this case, the Government tested the theory of how much prejudice has to be demonstrated. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: With regard to a couple of points made --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: May I ask one other question? The 5000 that have been detained, for how long?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, it varies from one day to 120 days, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But do we don't -- we don't have any notion of what the average period is?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, the average according to the marshall is something in excess of five days per person.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: I -- I believe counsel that counsel for the respondent indicated that even if the Government won this decision, it wouldn't alter the number of witnesses retained.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, I don't know where -- I don't understand how the respondent can make that claim. It seems to me inconceivable in light of cases like (Inaudible) where the Government has to detain 39 material witnesses for an excess of two weeks because of a decision and expressly on those facts by the Ninth Circuit. How could it be that if the Government altered its position, a number of illegal aliens wouldn't be reduced?

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: How about the suggestion that the witnesses be retained for simply long enough to let defense counsel interview them and then let the Government deport at its peril?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I mean, that sounds good in theory, Your Honor, but the reality is that we simply can't get defense counsel there and ready to go contrary to what respondents suggest.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, but if an opportunity were provided and they didn't take advantage of it, I suppose that's too bad.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That may, well -- yes, Your Honor. I think that's probably true although we don't -- I mean, you have to set specific deadlines and the Ninth Circuit at this point has been disinclined to say anything less than 10 days.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: All right, but let's suppose there are. I mean what's the matter with that then, with the shorter time and then yat point, Mr. Phillips, that -- that page 30 of the read brief, they give some figures about the number of cases and they say there were only 11 Mendez-Rodriguez motions filed in a total oou could deport at your peril, I suppose?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, if -- if we thought it would work and then we could in fact deport at our peril and given them two days, I just think we're going to have trouble if that -- having that enforced in the Ninth Circuit.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, you're certainly acting at your peril now. You don't say that you can just -- well, I guess you do. Your submission really is that if you -- once you interview them and that Government makes a decision that they would have no exculpatory evidence that you can deport them and -- and as long as you act in good faith, that's the end of the matter, no matter what the showing is on the other side.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Ultimately that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: That's your first submission.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I mean, we then have to make that kind of a determination with regard to each of the one million illegal aliens who are captured.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Have you ever had the Court agree with you on that, that first position?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Who?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: The Ninth Circuit holds that if we make a determination, that there is no connection in -- in time or space between the illegal alien and a criminal defendant, then we can --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: No one has ever -- you say on the facts of this case, if you make a good faith judgment that even though they were present on the spot, that they would have no evidence, that's the end of the matter no matter what kind of a showing the other side is.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Sure, what we're suggesting though is that there is not a demonstrable difference -- yes, that is our submission but that's not demonstrably different from the judgment --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Suppose we rejected that, what's your next fallback?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Is that -- is that respondent under any circumstances must demonstrate with some concreteness the materiality of the witnesses that were lost which they cannot do in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: And -- and is -- is that rule extended in any circuit that you know of?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I believe the Fifth Circuit adapts a rule very much like that.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Do you think that's the rule the Fifth Circuit adapts?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I think so, Your Honor, because in the Avila-Dominguez case, those -- those witnesses were witnesses to the crime at the time they were arrested and the Court held that they could be -- that they could be sent away.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: So you -- so, if you would be acting at your peril in deporting witnesses under the Fifth Circuit rule?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Certainly, to the extent there is a review, but -- I mean we don't -- we don't, you know wither from the notion that there'll be judicial review of our determination. I think we'll be right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Do you regularly take that risk in the Fifth Circuit now, or do you hold everybody?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: No, we regularly take that risk, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: In the -- under the Fifth Circuit rule?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, once we make an evaluation and if we think there is no material exculpatory evidence, we will deport. The -- we won't keep more than several.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Because the rule is not that different from the Ninth Circuit rule, you don't think it's so strict that you can't take that risk.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, I mean, it's not my decision to take that risk but the --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, it's somebody's decision.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's true, the Border Patrol agents and the Fifth Circuit have on occasion released illegal aliens especially in circumstances such like the (Inaudible) position -- situation where you have a large number of illegal aliens all of whom seem to say the same thing. We don't detain all 39 of them in the Fifth Circuit, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: My question -- my question really is that if you are -- if you don't -- if we do not agree with your first submission, would the Government regularly -- and I suppose you had to operate under the Fifth Circuit rule -- would the Government regularly release a lot of witnesses that it would not release under the Ninth Circuit rule?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well then, there would certainly be some instances where there is clear inculpatory statements made by the -- by the defendant at the time of his arrest and where all other statements that are sworn by the illegal aliens are cumulative, we will almost assuredly release some illegal aliens.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen. The case is submitted. We'll hear arguments next in --
